label: calendar-week-alert-dropdown

source.title: How to set up alerts in Calendar on Mac | Apple Support
source.downloadedFormat: itag 18 (mp4, 640x360, h264) via yt-dlp android client — highest format available without a GVS PO token
source.method: yt-dlp (android player client) + ffmpeg -ss 46 -t 12 to cut, ffmpeg -vf fps=2 to extract frames

interaction: Real macOS Calendar app, Week view, an existing event's detail popover open. The clip shows clicking the 'alert' field's dropdown control: the option list is open (frame 1), the cursor hovers/presses the target row '2 hours before' which highlights (~frame 13, blue highlight on the row), then the dropdown collapses and the alert field commits to the new value (frame 24). This is the same control family (Week-view event popover, click-to-open dropdown, click-to-commit, collapse) that the demo's 'click empty slot -> new event -> type title' story exercises, though this specific clip is an existing-event alert edit rather than a from-scratch event creation — closest available real footage without driving the live app or logging in.
note: This session's computer-use/AppleScript control of the local Finder/Calendar apps was denied/blocked (no interactive user available to approve GUI-automation permission dialogs in this subagent context), so rung 1 (native-local screencapture -v of the real installed Calendar app) and computer-use driving were not available. Fell back to rung 3 of the motion ladder (docs/reference-and-asset-sourcing.md Part 1.5): an official Apple vendor video frame-mined with yt-dlp. Only itag 18 (360p) was obtainable without a GVS PO token — YouTube blocked higher-resolution web/ios/tv client formats for this session. Use for pacing/easing/press-state calibration; do not treat pixel fidelity below 360p source as authoritative for cropped detail.
assetDecision: Frame-mined stills/clip from a public, official Apple YouTube video. Reference-only for pacing/state-transition calibration; do not copy the video's pixels, event content, or map imagery into any shipped surface.
